items.title: Joint communication
items.contentHtml:
Communication with the knee joint

  • The Baker's cyst is a distention of the semimembranosus-gastrocnemia bursa that communicates with the knee joint cavity through a pedicle.
  • This communication occurs by a one-way valve mechanism: synovial fluid passes from the joint to the bursa during flexion, but does not return with the extension, which favors progressive accumulation.
  • The presence of this connection explains why the cyst appears almost exclusively in contexts of chronic joint effusion, since the excess synovial fluid finds an escape route to the popliteal bursa.

items.title: Chronic joint effusion
items.contentHtml:
Associated with chronic joint effusion

  • The Baker's cyst is secondary to any process that generates chronic joint effusion of the knee, such as osteoarthritis (more common) or rheumatoid arthritis.
  • Sustained intra-articular hypertension forces the passage of synovial fluid into the popliteal bursa, so the cyst is a marker of underlying joint pathology.
  • In clinical practice, the presence of a Baker's cyst should require a search for underlying joint disease; Exclusive treatment of the cyst without addressing the cause has a high recurrence rate.

items.title: Rupture vs DVT
items.contentHtml:
Rupture simulates deep vein thrombosis

  • The rupture of the Baker's cyst causes extravasation of inflammatory synovial fluid into the muscular planes of the calf, generating pain, edema, erythema and local heat.
  • This clinical picture is indistinguishable from a deep vein thrombosis (DVT), constituting the main differential diagnosis.
  • To differentiate them, the Doppler ultrasound which, in addition to ruling out DVT, confirms the presence of the cyst and the extent of the fluid. The Homan sign (calf pain when dorsiflexing the foot) is not specific and can be positive in both entities.

items.title: Doppler ultrasound
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis confirmed with Doppler ultrasound

  • Doppler ultrasound is the imaging test of choice to confirm Baker's cyst and rule out deep vein thrombosis (DVT), since it simultaneously evaluates cystic morphology and venous patency.
  • Key ultrasound finding: either anechoic or hypoechoic image delimited in the popliteal fossa, with posterior acoustic reinforcement, which may present septa or echogenic material if there is a complication (rupture or hemorrhage).
  • Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is more sensitive to detect small or atypical cysts, but it is not used routinely due to its cost; Ultrasound is sufficient in most cases.

items.title: Inflammatory synovial fluid
items.contentHtml:
Content: inflammatory synovial fluid

  • The content of the Baker's cyst is inflammatory synovial fluid, which can be clear, cloudy or hemorrhagic depending on the underlying joint pathology.
  • In osteoarthritis the fluid is usually mechanical (clear, yellowish, with less than 2000 cells per mm³), while in rheumatoid arthritis it is inflammatory (cloudy, with more than 2000 cells per mm³, predominantly polymorphonuclear).
  • The presence of monosodium urate crystals or calcium pyrophosphate indicates an associated microcrystalline arthropathy (gout or pseudogout).

items.foco: More common in osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis
items.title: Etiological association
items.contentHtml:
More common in osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis

  • The Baker's cyst is more common in patients with osteoarthritis of the knee (main cause, especially in those over 50 years of age) and in rheumatoid arthritis (chronic inflammatory disease).
  • In osteoarthritis, the effusion is mechanical and the cyst It is usually incidental; In rheumatoid arthritis, the cyst may be larger and more symptomatic due to increased production of inflammatory synovial fluid. Other less common causes include psoriatic arthritis, gout, pseudogout, and repetitive trauma.

items.foco: May extend to the gastrocnemius muscle
items.title: Extension to the gastrocnemius
items.contentHtml:
It can extend to the gastrocnemius muscle

  • The Baker's cyst can dissect the muscular planes and extend towards the gastrocnemius muscle, especially when it ruptures or when the cyst is large.
  • The extension to the gastrocnemius occurs along the fascia that separates the medial and lateral heads of the muscle, and can reach up to the middle third of the calf.
  • Ultrasound shows a liquid collection that appears between the muscle fibers; This finding can be confused with a hematoma or abscess, but communication with the knee and the absence of Doppler flow confirm the diagnosis.

items.title: Foucher's sign
items.contentHtml:
Foucher's sign on physical examination

  • The Foucher's sign (or transillumination sign) is examined with the patient in a prone position and the knee in full extension; When palpating the popliteal fossa, the tumor becomes more tense and painful.
  • When the knee is flexed (generally at 45°), the cyst becomes softer and may disappear on palpation, because the fluid refluxes into the joint (positive Foucher sign).
  • This sign has moderate sensitivity and is useful in physical examination, but does not rule out the cyst if it is negative; Confirmation always requires imaging (Doppler ultrasound).

items.foco: Tratamiento conservador inicial: reposo y hielo
items.title: Tratamiento conservador
items.contentHtml:
Tratamiento conservador inicial: reposo y hielo

  • En quistes de Baker asintomáticos o levemente sintomáticos, el manejo inicial es conservador: reposo relativo, hielo local 15-20 minutos cada 4-6 horas, elevación de la extremidad y antiinflamatorios no esteroideos (AINES) orales si hay dolor.
  • La compresión elástica con venda o media puede ayudar a reducir el edema, pero debe evitarse la compresión excesiva que pueda comprometer la circulación venosa.
  • El tratamiento conservador es efectivo en la mayoría de los casos, especialmente cuando se trata la patología articular de base (por ejemplo, fisioterapia en artrosis o modificación del tratamiento en artritis reumatoide).

items.title: Ultrasound-guided drainage
items.contentHtml:
Ultrasound-guided drainage in symptomatic cases

  • Ultrasound-guided drainage is indicated in symptomatic Baker cysts (pain, functional limitation, neurovascular compression) that do not respond to conservative treatment.
  • Percutaneous aspiration of the cyst contents with a fine needle (puncture-evacuation) is performed under real-time ultrasound visualization to avoid vascular lesions. (popliteal artery and popliteal vein).
  • After aspiration, corticosteroids (for example, triamcinolone 40 mg) can be instilled to reduce local inflammation and reduce recurrence in the short term.

items.foco: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection as an option
items.title: Intra-articular corticosteroids
items.contentHtml:
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection as an option

  • Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (in the knee, not directly into the cyst) is a therapeutic option to reduce synovial effusion and, consequently, the size of the Baker's cyst.
  • Typically triamcinolone 40 mg or methylprednisolone 40 is administered. mg in the knee joint, after confirming that there is no infection (do not perform if septic arthritis is suspected).
  • This approach is especially useful in patients with rheumatoid arthritis or osteoarthritis with active effusion; The response is usually rapid but temporary, so it must be combined with the treatment of the underlying disease.

items.foco: High recurrence if underlying cause is not treated
items.title: Recurrence and underlying cause
items.contentHtml:
High recurrence if underlying cause is not treated

  • The recurrence rate of Baker's cyst is high (>50% in clinical series) if the underlying cause of chronic joint effusion is not addressed (osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, etc.).
  • Even after guided drainage or injection of corticosteroids, the cyst tends to reappear as long as intra-articular hypertension persists.
  • Therefore, the pillar of long-term treatment is optimal management of the underlying joint disease: physiotherapy, activity modification, disease-modifying drugs in rheumatoid arthritis, or surgery (arthroplasty) in advanced osteoarthritis.
